next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

The pool was empty except for Lena.

Margot settled into the coaching chair on the observation deck and woke the session display. It was six forty-five a.m. The facility hadn't come online yet. The sprinters would not start until eight. The calibration bays were dark. Below the glass, the pool lay flat under the overheads, ten lanes of hard blue light. Lena stood on the far deck pulling on her cap. When she tucked her hair back, the port at the base of her skull showed for a second: a small round disc, skin-colored, then hidden again.

Margot strapped the feed interface to her right wrist. The pads found her pulse and warmed. She entered the athlete ID. The set was standard aerobic. The pace target was 1:04 per hundred. It was clean enough to live inside.

She connected.

Air came first. The deck was cool on Lena's bare feet. There was tightness through the shoulders from yesterday. There was also a dry ache behind the eyes that still shouldn't have been altitude after six weeks. Then Lena dove and the water closed over Margot like a second skin.

Lena's stroke always hit her hardest at the catch. That first loaded grip on the water ran up Margot's forearm and into her jaw. Her mouth went wet every time. The turn at the wall drove heat into her thighs, into muscles she hadn't trusted in years. This was what she used the feed for. It was not insight. It was not coaching. It was the hit when somebody else's body found the right line and hers answered anyway.

The first hundred came in 1:03.8. The second came in 1:04.1. Her heart rate held at 162. Stroke rate hel...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...ng physical she might be able to outrun. The Brazilian gymnast kept reading the paragraph about baseline markers, lips moving over the same three lines.

By the time Jin-seo checked her watch, only six binders were still closed.

"I'll be here through Thursday," she said. Her thumb touched the face of the watch, the same small check from the start. "If you need time, take it. If you want to ask questions privately, do that. No one is being marched to the calibration bay this morning."

She gathered her portfolio. The two men from Meridian rose and began collecting the signed binders with practiced efficiency. One wore thin surgical gloves. The latex flashed pale each time he bent to pick one up, and Kai had to look away.

His own binder lay open on his lap. At some point he had opened it without noticing. The signature line was blank. Below it, in smaller type, were the cross-references Tomas had touched: sponsorship compliance, support obligations, data-use addenda. Kai Brennan waited there in print, neat and harmless-looking.

His left wrist gave a deep, grinding throb inside the brace. Bone knitting too fast. Tissue pulling itself together under instructions he had never agreed to and could not revise. He closed the binder and pressed his good hand flat over the cover until the pulse in his wrist and the pulse in his palm stopped feeling like separate things.

Outside the conference room windows, the Alps stood white under a sky that was starting to clear. Wind peeled snow off the high ridges in thin sheets, something immense and indifferent moving just beyond the glass.
